title: Convergent Evolution of Information:
content:
When I was new to computational biology, my masters thesis supervisor casually mentioned that nature contains mechanisms that look almost offensively “engineered”: a motor on a bacterium, assembly-like protein complexes, feedback controllers that resemble circuits. The remark was made to simply imply that the objective function is the same.

I do not mean this in a mystical “the universe wants X” sense. I mean it in the optimization-theory sense. If two systems must solve the same class of problems under the same class of constraints, then the space of viable solutions collapses. Different starting points will still drift toward the same attractors. In biology we call this convergent evolution. In information systems (whether biochemical, neural, social, or digital), I think there is an analogous phenomenon: a convergent evolution of information.

Information here is not merely “data.” It is the ability to preserve a pattern, to make a prediction, to coordinate action, to discriminate signal from noise, to keep a model stable enough to be useful and flexible enough to remain true. Under scarcity, noise, and uncertainty, certain strategies keep reappearing. They are not copied. They are rediscovered.

Start with the simplest pressure: error. Any system that stores and transmits information must contend with corruption. Biology invented redundancy, proofreading, and repair. DNA replication is not just copying; it is copying with correction. In our engineered world we build the same logic into protocols and memory systems: checksums, parity, retries, forward error correction. The substrate changes; the mathematics of robustness remains.

Then there is the pressure of allocation. A system has limited resources: energy, time, bandwidth, attention. And it must decide where to spend them. Modern AI expresses this explicitly with normalization schemes that distribute weight over competing options. Softmax, for instance, is not an exotic invention; it is the same functional form as the Boltzmann distribution, which shows up whenever a system assigns probabilities under tradeoffs. Biology is full of “softmax-like” compromises: binding affinities, competitive inhibition, pathway selection. Again, the details differ, but the signature is recognizable: exponential weighting, normalization, selection.

Recognition is another pressure that forces convergence. The immune system faces a combinatorial nightmare: an effectively open-ended space of possible pathogens, with the added constraint that it must not destroy the self. In machine learning we face a different nightmare: high-dimensional spaces where “what matters” is sparse, shifting, and often adversarial. The immune system’s self/non-self discrimination is not literally a contrastive loss written down in closed form, but the family resemblance is hard to ignore. Both are selection mechanisms under uncertainty. Both must become good at saying “this belongs” and “this does not” without a perfect model of the world.

Even the now-standard AI pipeline—pre-train broadly, specialize through fine-tuning, then align through higher-order constraints and feedback—begins to look less like a cultural accident and more like an engineering rediscovery of a general developmental pattern. Biology starts with priors and rough structure, generates variation, and then sculpts behavior through layers of selection. Immunity, in particular, begins with a germline repertoire, explores through diversification, and then prunes through selection and tolerance checks. The correspondence is not exact, and I do not want to flatten biology into a metaphor for GPUs. But the architectural rhyme is the point: broad exposure first, specialization second, constraint and social compatibility third.

This is why the remark about “machines in nature” matters. The bacterial flagellar motor is not a poetic analogy; it is what you get when you need to convert chemical gradients into directed motion at microscopic scales. It is rotary because rotational mechanics is a stable way to convert continuous energy into continuous work. Humans build turbines and motors for the same reason. Nature did not copy us; we did not copy nature. Physics forced both of us into the same corner.

Humans engineered pulse-echo ranging and called it sonar. Bats evolved pulse-echo ranging and we called it echolocation. Once more, the substrate differs but the informational problem does not: infer a world from delayed reflections under noise. When you need a map and you cannot see, you will eventually build a way to query the world and interpret the returning signal.

Memory exposes perhaps the clearest case of convergent informational architecture. In AI we now speak of retrieval-augmented generation: keep fast, always-on knowledge in the model’s parameters, and keep a larger, cheaper, more updateable store outside the model, retrieved on demand. People treat this as a new trick, but it is a rediscovery of an old constraint: keeping everything “hot” is too expensive.

Biology, too, separates fast response from scalable storage. There are metabolically costly, always-ready mechanisms, and there are quieter forms of memory that can be reactivated and expanded when the environment demands. Human beings do this as well: habit and skill live in one place; external notes, books, and now search engines live in another. Scarcity forces architecture. Two-tier memory is not a fashion; it is an answer.

This is where I find the phrase “objective function” genuinely useful, even if it is borrowed. Selection behaves like a blind optimizer. It does not know what it is doing, but it systematically rewards persistence, efficiency, and robustness within a niche. Engineering does the same thing with explicit criteria: minimize energy for a given performance, maximize reliability, compress representation, improve control. The world’s constraints are the same, so the solutions converge.

This does not mean every similarity is profound. We should be cautious with grand claims of “mathematical identity.” It is easy to become intoxicated by analogy and start seeing Transformers in T-cells and gradient descent in every biological process. But even after we strip away the hype, something remains: the repeated appearance of the same informational motifs.

If the convergent evolution of information is real, then AI is not an alien intrusion into nature. It is one more substrate on which old problems get solved again. This realization can be sobering and liberating at the same time. Sobering, because it suggests that many of our “breakthroughs” are constrained rediscoveries; liberating, because it suggests that intelligence is not magic but structure.

The practical question is what we do with this mirror. When engineers deliberately build what nature built blindly, we are no longer excused by ignorance. We get to choose the objective function. Optimization is powerful, but it is indifferent. It will happily maximize the wrong thing.

So perhaps the deepest conclusion of this convergence is not technical but ethical: if informational architectures are inevitable, then meaning and freedom are not. Those remain the human variables. However, if our own social and cognitive structures are also subject to the "convergent evolution of information," to what extent is our "choice" of objective functions actually free, and to what extent is it just another attractor driven by the scarcity and competition of our environment?
